title: Definite integrals: areas and volumes of revolution
summary: This lesson turns the definite integral into a tool for measuring space: the area under a curve, the area trapped between two curves, and the volume of a solid formed by spinning a region about the x-axis. You will read the definite integral as a signed area, compute the area between two curves as $\int_a^b(f-g)\,dx$ (upper minus lower), find a volume of revolution about the x-axis with $V=\pi\int_a^b y^2\,dx$, meet integration by substitution for the reversal step, and use the GDC to evaluate any of these numerically. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards marks, using IB Mathematics method (M) and accuracy (A) conventions — because in an exam, how you show your working decides how many marks you keep.
learningObjectives: Interpret $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ as the signed area between the curve, the x-axis and the lines $x=a$, $x=b$, and find total area with $\int_a^b|f(x)|\,dx$.
Find the area enclosed between two curves as $\int_a^b(\text{upper}-\text{lower})\,dx = \int_a^b(f(x)-g(x))\,dx$.
Find the volume of a solid of revolution about the x-axis using $V=\pi\int_a^b y^2\,dx$.
Reverse a composite function using integration by substitution in basic cases.
Use the GDC to evaluate definite integrals, areas and volumes, storing intersection points to preserve accuracy.
Present working so that method marks (M), accuracy marks (A) and follow-through (FT) protect the final answer.

simpleExplanation.title: Adding Up Infinitely Thin Slices
simpleExplanation.summary: A definite integral adds up an infinite number of infinitely thin slices. Line the slices up under a curve and you get an area; spin each slice into a thin disc and stack them, and you get a volume.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a loaf of bread cut into extremely thin slices. To find the area of the loaf's side profile, add up the heights of all the slices. Now imagine each slice is actually a thin circular disc — spin the profile around a skewer through the middle (the x-axis) and every slice becomes a coin of radius $y$ and area $\pi y^2$. Stacking all those coins gives the volume of the solid. Integration is the machine that adds up infinitely many of these slices exactly.
steps: Sketch the region on your GDC and identify the limits $a$ and $b$ — often the x-values where curves meet.
Choose the right integral: area under a curve $\int_a^b y\,dx$; area between curves $\int_a^b(\text{upper}-\text{lower})\,dx$; volume about the x-axis $\pi\int_a^b y^2\,dx$ (remember to square $y$).
Evaluate — by hand for a simple power, or with the GDC's numerical integrator for anything harder. Store exact intersection values, don't retype rounded ones.
State the answer to 3 significant figures unless told otherwise, and include units (area in units$^2$, volume in units$^3$).

keyConcepts: area between two curves (upper minus lower)
volume of revolution about the x-axis

flashcards.back: The signed area between $y=f(x)$, the x-axis and the lines $x=a$ and $x=b$. Regions below the x-axis count as negative.

flashcards.front: How do you find the TOTAL (physical) area when a curve is partly below the x-axis?
flashcards.back: Integrate the absolute value: $\int_a^b|f(x)|\,dx$. On the GDC this makes the parts below the axis count positively before summing.

flashcards.front: What is the area between two curves $f$ and $g$ on $[a,b]$ where $f\ge g$?
flashcards.back: $A=\int_a^b(f(x)-g(x))\,dx$ — the integral of the upper curve minus the lower curve.

flashcards.front: First step for the area between two curves when limits are not given?
flashcards.back: Solve $f(x)=g(x)$ (GDC solver or intersection tool) to find the x-values where the curves meet. These become your limits $a$ and $b$.

flashcards.front: What is the volume of revolution about the x-axis for $y=f(x)$ on $[a,b]$?
flashcards.back: $V=\pi\int_a^b y^2\,dx = \pi\int_a^b [f(x)]^2\,dx$. Each disc has radius $y$ and area $\pi y^2$.

flashcards.front: Common trap: what gets squared in the volume formula?
flashcards.back: You square $y$, then integrate, then multiply by $\pi$: $V=\pi\int y^2\,dx$. It is NOT $\pi\int y\,dx$ and NOT $\left(\pi\int y\,dx\right)^2$.

flashcards.front: What is integration by substitution used for?
flashcards.back: Reversing the chain rule. Let $u$ be the 'inside' function; replace $\mathrm{d}x$ using $\mathrm{d}u=\frac{du}{dx}\,dx$, integrate in $u$, then convert limits or substitute back.

flashcards.front: When you substitute in a DEFINITE integral, what happens to the limits?
flashcards.back: Change them to the corresponding $u$-values (evaluate $u$ at each x-limit). Then you never need to convert back to $x$.

flashcards.front: Why store intersection points in the GDC instead of writing them down rounded?
flashcards.back: Retyping a rounded value (e.g. $1.63$ instead of $1.6308\ldots$) introduces error that can cost the final accuracy mark. Store and recall the full-precision value.

flashcards.front: What units does a volume of revolution have if $x$ and $y$ are in centimetres?
flashcards.back: Cubic centimetres, cm$^3$. Areas are units$^2$; volumes are units$^3$. Always check for units in context problems.

quickCheck.prompt: The volume when $y=f(x)$ on $[a,b]$ is rotated $360^\circ$ about the x-axis is:
The area enclosed between two curves on $[a,b]$ is found by integrating:
A student writes $V=\int_0^3 x^2\,dx$ for a volume of revolution about the x-axis where $y=x$. What have they forgotten?
To evaluate $\int_1^4 \dfrac{\ln x}{x^2+1}\,dx$ on an AI HL calculator paper, the best approach is to:
options: Use the GDC's numerical (definite) integration feature
Estimate the answer by eye from a sketch
quickCheck.answer: Use the GDC's numerical (definite) integration feature

quickCheck.prompt: In the substitution $\int_0^2 2x\,e^{x^2}\,dx$ with $u=x^2$, the new limits become:

faq.q: Do I have to integrate by hand on Paper 1?
faq.a: AI HL papers are technology-active: your GDC is expected throughout. For a simple power like $\int_0^4 x\,dx$ you may integrate by hand and it is fast to do so, but for anything awkward you set up the correct definite integral and evaluate it numerically on the GDC. Marks are awarded for the correct set-up and the correct value, not for hand-computation.

faq.q: What if the two curves cross inside my interval?
faq.a: Then 'upper' and 'lower' swap partway along. Either split the region at the crossing point and add the separate areas, or — much simpler on a GDC — integrate the absolute value of the difference: $A=\int_a^b|f(x)-g(x)|\,dx$. This handles the swap automatically and always returns a positive total area.

faq.q: When do I actually need integration by substitution?
faq.a: When the integrand is a composite times (a multiple of) the derivative of its inside — for example $\int 2x\,e^{x^2}\,dx$, where the $2x$ is the derivative of the inside $x^2$. Substituting $u=x^2$ turns it into $\int e^u\,du$. On a calculator paper you can often skip it and integrate numerically, but recognising the structure is examinable HL content.

faq.q: How do I get the volume when I rotate the region between two curves about the x-axis?
faq.a: Use the washer idea: subtract the inner solid from the outer, $V=\pi\int_a^b\big([f(x)]^2-[g(x)]^2\big)\,dx$, where $f$ is the outer (larger) radius and $g$ the inner. Note you square each function separately before subtracting — it is NOT $\pi\int(f-g)^2\,dx$.

sections.content: So far you have treated integration as the reverse of differentiation. Now you will put it to geometric work. The definite integral $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ measures a signed area, and from that one idea everything in this lesson follows: the area under a curve, the area trapped between two curves, and — by spinning a region into a solid — a volume of revolution. In this technology-active course the GDC does the heavy arithmetic, so your marks come from setting up the right integral and reading the result correctly. As in every calculation topic, each worked example below is laid out exactly the way our engine marks your own practice: the method is on the page, mark by mark.
The definite integral and the area under a curve

The definite integral $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ gives the signed area of the region bounded by $y=f(x)$, the x-axis and the vertical lines $x=a$ and $x=b$. 'Signed' means any part of the region lying below the x-axis contributes a negative amount. When the curve stays above the axis on $[a,b]$, the definite integral is exactly the geometric area. When it dips below, and you want the total physical area, integrate the absolute value of the function instead.

Signed area: $A=\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$

Total (physical) area, allowing for parts below the axis: $A=\int_a^b |f(x)|\,dx$

On the GDC, both are one-line calculations: the numerical integration feature evaluates $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ directly, and wrapping the integrand in an absolute value handles a curve that changes sign. This is the standard method in AI HL — you rarely need to find where the curve crosses the axis by hand.
The area between two curves

To find the area enclosed between two curves $y=f(x)$ and $y=g(x)$, integrate the difference between the upper curve and the lower curve across the interval where they enclose the region. Subtracting the lower function strips away the area beneath it, leaving precisely the area trapped between the two. The limits are usually the x-coordinates where the curves intersect.

Area between curves (with $f\ge g$ on $[a,b]$):

$A=\int_a^b\big(\text{upper}-\text{lower}\big)\,dx = \int_a^b\big(f(x)-g(x)\big)\,dx$

items: Graph both functions on the GDC and find the intersection points — these x-values are your limits $a$ and $b$.
The graph shows at a glance which curve is 'upper' and which is 'lower' inside the region.
Set up the integral as upper minus lower, then evaluate with the GDC's numerical integrator.
If the curves cross within the interval, split the integral at the crossing point or integrate $|f(x)-g(x)|$ to keep the total positive.
Store the intersection values in the calculator's memory; do not retype rounded versions.

sections.question:
Let $f(x)=x+1$ and $g(x)=0.5e^{x}$.
(a) Find the x-coordinates of the points where the graphs of $f$ and $g$ meet.
(b) Hence find the area of the region enclosed by the two graphs. [6]
sections.solution:
**(a) Find the intersections.** Set $f(x)=g(x)$:
$x+1=0.5e^{x}$.  [M1: equate the functions]
Graphing $y_1=x+1$ and $y_2=0.5e^{x}$ on the GDC and using the intersection tool gives
$x\approx-0.826$ and $x\approx1.63$.  [A1][A1: both x-values]
Store these as $A=-0.8262\ldots$ and $B=1.6308\ldots$ to keep full accuracy.

**(b) Set up and evaluate the area.** Between the intersections the line $f(x)=x+1$ lies above the curve $g(x)=0.5e^{x}$, so
$A=\displaystyle\int_{A}^{B}\big((x+1)-0.5e^{x}\big)\,dx$.  [M1: upper minus lower, with their limits]
Evaluating with the GDC's numerical integrator (using the stored $A$ and $B$):
$A\approx1.0965\ldots$  [A1: correct value]
$A\approx1.10$ (3 s.f.).  [A1: correct rounding]

sections.content: Always sketch both curves on the GDC before integrating. The sketch hands you the limits (the intersections) and shows which curve is on top, killing the most common error — subtracting in the wrong order and getting a negative 'area'. If your area comes out negative, you have swapped upper and lower; take the positive value and note the swap.
Volume of revolution about the x-axis

A solid of revolution is formed by rotating a region through $360^\circ$ about an axis. Rotate the region under $y=f(x)$ about the x-axis and each vertical slice sweeps out a thin disc: its radius is the height of the curve, $y$, so its area is $\pi y^2$ and its volume is $\pi y^2\,dx$. Adding up all the discs — that is, integrating — gives the total volume. The single most important habit here is to square $y$ before integrating.

Volume of revolution about the x-axis, $x=a$ to $x=b$:

$V=\pi\int_a^b y^2\,dx = \pi\int_a^b \big[f(x)\big]^2\,dx$

items: Square the function first, then integrate, then multiply by $\pi$: $V=\pi\int_a^b y^2\,dx$.
The radius of each disc is the height $y=f(x)$; that is why the integrand is $y^2$, not $y$.
For a region between two curves rotated about the x-axis, use the washer form $V=\pi\int_a^b\big([f(x)]^2-[g(x)]^2\big)\,dx$ — square each separately.
The GDC evaluates $\int_a^b y^2\,dx$ numerically; just remember to multiply the result by $\pi$.

sections.question: The region bounded by the curve $y=x^{2}+1$, the x-axis and the lines $x=0$ and $x=2$ is rotated $360^\circ$ about the x-axis. Find the exact volume of the solid formed. [4]
sections.solution:
**Set up the volume integral.** Rotating about the x-axis:
$V=\pi\displaystyle\int_0^2 y^2\,dx=\pi\int_0^2 (x^2+1)^2\,dx$.  [M1: $V=\pi\int y^2\,dx$ with the correct $y$]

**Expand and integrate.** $(x^2+1)^2=x^4+2x^2+1$, so
$V=\pi\displaystyle\int_0^2\big(x^4+2x^2+1\big)\,dx=\pi\left[\frac{x^5}{5}+\frac{2x^3}{3}+x\right]_0^2$.  [M1: expand the square and integrate]

**Evaluate the limits.**
$V=\pi\left(\frac{32}{5}+\frac{16}{3}+2\right)=\pi\left(\frac{96+80+30}{15}\right)=\frac{206\pi}{15}$.  [A1: correct exact expression]
$V=\dfrac{206\pi}{15}\approx43.1$.  [A1: correct value, 3 s.f.]

sections.content: The single biggest volume-of-revolution error is forgetting to square $y$, or dropping the $\pi$. Read the formula as three actions in order — square, integrate, times $\pi$ — and check both are present in your working before you press ENTER. On a calculator paper you can compute $\int_a^b y^2\,dx$ numerically and then multiply by $\pi$ at the very end.
Integration by substitution

Substitution reverses the chain rule. When the integrand is a composite function multiplied by (a constant times) the derivative of its inside, let $u$ be that inside function. Replacing $\mathrm{d}x$ using $\mathrm{d}u=\frac{du}{dx}\,dx$ turns the integral into a simpler one in $u$. For a definite integral, the cleanest approach is to convert the limits to $u$-values as you go — then you never have to substitute back.

If $u=g(x)$ then $\mathrm{d}u=g'(x)\,dx$, and

$\displaystyle\int_a^b f\big(g(x)\big)\,g'(x)\,dx=\int_{g(a)}^{g(b)} f(u)\,du$

sections.question: Find $\displaystyle\int_0^2 2x\,e^{x^{2}}\,dx$ using the substitution $u=x^{2}$. Give your answer in exact form. [4]
sections.solution:
**Choose the substitution.** Let $u=x^{2}$, so $\dfrac{du}{dx}=2x$, giving $\mathrm{d}u=2x\,dx$.  [M1: substitution with $du=2x\,dx$]

**Change the limits.** When $x=0$, $u=0^2=0$; when $x=2$, $u=2^2=4$.  [A1: new limits $0$ and $4$]

**Rewrite and integrate.** The $2x\,dx$ becomes $\mathrm{d}u$, so
$\displaystyle\int_0^2 2x\,e^{x^{2}}\,dx=\int_0^4 e^{u}\,du=\big[e^{u}\big]_0^4$.  [M1: integral transformed correctly in $u$]

**Evaluate.** $=e^{4}-e^{0}=e^{4}-1\approx53.6$.  [A1: exact value $e^4-1$]

sections.content: Two habits save marks with substitution. First, once you change to $u$, change the LIMITS to $u$-values immediately — that is why the $0$ and $2$ became $0$ and $4$ above. Second, if you keep the original x-limits by mistake, you must substitute back to $x$ before evaluating; mixing $u$-limits with an answer in $x$ is a classic slip. On a calculator paper, you can always confirm the exact answer numerically with the GDC's definite integrator.
Using the GDC to evaluate definite integrals and volumes

Your GDC is the workhorse of this topic. Its numerical (definite) integration feature evaluates $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ for any function you can type — no algebra required — which is exactly what areas and volumes reduce to. For an area between curves, integrate the difference (or its absolute value). For a volume about the x-axis, integrate $y^2$ and multiply the result by $\pi$. Two accuracy habits matter: find intersection limits with the graph or solver and STORE them at full precision rather than retyping rounded values, and restrict any search to a sensible domain, since lengths, areas and volumes must be positive.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Forgetting to square $y$ in a volume** — the disc area is $\pi y^2$, so the integrand is $y^2$. Writing $\pi\int y\,dx$ finds an area times $\pi$, not a volume.
**Dropping the factor of $\pi$** in a volume of revolution — the formula is $V=\pi\int_a^b y^2\,dx$; leaving out the $\pi$ loses the final accuracy mark.
**Subtracting the curves the wrong way round** — area between curves is upper minus lower. Getting a negative answer signals a swap; take the positive value.
**Using $\pi\int(f-g)^2\,dx$ for a washer** — you must square each function separately: $\pi\int([f]^2-[g]^2)\,dx$. These are not equal.
**Retyping rounded intersection points** — using $1.63$ instead of the stored $1.6308\ldots$ corrupts the final answer. Store and recall full precision.
**Keeping x-limits after substituting to $u$** — either convert the limits to $u$-values, or substitute back to $x$ before evaluating. Never mix the two.
**Dropping units** — areas in units$^2$, volumes in units$^3$. A bare number in a context problem can cost the final accuracy mark.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

In an exam the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working, using the IB Mathematics conventions. A method mark (M) rewards a correct approach even if the arithmetic later slips; an accuracy mark (A) rewards a correct result and is normally dependent on the method mark being earned. Follow-through (FT) means a correct final step carried out on your own earlier (even wrong) value still scores, and equivalent correct forms — including an exact answer such as $8\pi$ or its decimal $25.1$ — are accepted. On this technology-active paper the GDC is allowed throughout. All of that protection exists only if your method is on the page. Study how each mark below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: The region bounded by $y=\sqrt{x}$, the x-axis, and $x=4$ is rotated $360^\circ$ about the x-axis. Find the volume of the solid formed. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

**Set up the volume integral.** Rotating about the x-axis, $V=\pi\displaystyle\int_0^4 y^2\,dx$, with limits $x=0$ (where $y=\sqrt{x}$ meets the x-axis) and $x=4$:
$V=\pi\displaystyle\int_0^4 (\sqrt{x})^2\,dx$.

**Simplify and integrate.** Since $(\sqrt{x})^2=x$,
$V=\pi\displaystyle\int_0^4 x\,dx=\pi\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^4$.

**Evaluate.**
$V=\pi\left(\frac{4^2}{2}-0\right)=\pi(8)=8\pi\approx25.1$.

**Final answer:** the volume of the solid is $8\pi\approx25.1$ (cubic units).

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks:**

- **M1 — volume set-up.** Awarded for using $V=\pi\int y^2\,dx$ about the x-axis with the correct limits $0$ and $4$. This is a METHOD mark: it stands even if the integration later slips.
- **M1 — square and integrate.** Awarded for using $y^2=(\sqrt{x})^2=x$ and integrating to $\pi\int_0^4 x\,dx$. A second method mark for correctly simplifying the integrand and setting up the integration.
- **A1 — correct antiderivative.** Awarded for $\pi\left[\dfrac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^4$. This accuracy mark depends on the method marks above.
- **A1 — final value.** Awarded for $8\pi\approx25.1$. Follow-through (FT) applies: a student who mis-simplified $y^2$ but then integrated and evaluated correctly on their own expression still earns this mark. Exact form $8\pi$ and the decimal $25.1$ are both accepted.

**Accept equivalent forms.** The engine marks the mathematics, not the formatting: it accepts $8\pi$, $25.1$, or $25.1$ cubic units, and does not require the $\pi$ to be evaluated. A GDC-only computation of $\pi\int_0^4 x\,dx=25.1\ldots$ scores full marks provided the set-up $\pi\int y^2\,dx$ is shown.

**Bottom line:** of the 4 marks, two are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip, and the final accuracy mark is shielded by follow-through. A student who writes only '$25.1$' with no set-up risks losing the two method marks if a number is wrong; a student who shows $\pi\int_0^4(\sqrt{x})^2\,dx$, the simplification to $\pi\int_0^4 x\,dx$, and the evaluation keeps those marks regardless.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with follow-through. So always show the set-up $V=\pi\int y^2\,dx$ with its limits, the squaring of $y$, the integration, and the substitution of the limits, ending with units. The method marks and follow-through are what protect your score when a final number slips — and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Where this leads

Every calculation here is one idea — the definite integral adds up infinitely many thin slices — dressed for three jobs: area under a curve, area between curves, and volume of revolution. Substitution simply lets you reverse composite functions so those integrals can be evaluated exactly, while the GDC handles anything too awkward for hand-integration. The same set-up-then-evaluate discipline carries straight into the modelling and kinematics questions that dominate Paper 2, where an integral of velocity gives distance and an integral of a rate gives a total. Master the habit — sketch, choose the right integral, square where a volume needs it, evaluate, and state units — and the rest of integral calculus becomes variations on a method you already own.
